behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Top candidate: Unit. Runner-up: Integration. Rejected Integration because the behavior text is purely algorithmic: "wait time ... doubles ... up to a configured maximum"; it can fail inside one backoff-calculation function with no module handoff. REJECTION-GATE for higher levels: no quoted need for real OS/network/subprocess, no AI/ML dependency, no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure `computeBackoff(attempt, baseDelay, maxDelay)` (or equivalent policy object method) across attempts 1..N, asserting exponential growth and cap clamping.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Top candidate: Integration. Runner-up: Unit. Rejected Unit because this behavior explicitly combines two concerns in one path: "identifies a rate-limit response" + "computes ... wait time," which is a module-boundary handoff risk (response classification -> retry policy). INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no required real OS/network/subprocess in the behavior text; stubbed responses are sufficient. No AI/ML or multi-step agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry orchestrator + rate-limit classifier + backoff policy with a stub transport returning a 429-like response (and optional headers). Assert it is recognized as quota exhaustion and the scheduled delay matches policy/header rules.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Top candidate: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejected System because the behavior does not require real HTTP/network conditions; quoted scope is "resends the original request" and "returns the successful result," which can be validated with in-process stubs plus controlled timers. This is a cross-module async orchestration failure boundary, not OS/network-specific.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers and a stub transport that returns rate-limit once, then success. Assert the transport receives the same request payload twice (original + retry) and `call()` resolves to the success value without exposing retry internals.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Top candidate: Integration. Runner-up: Unit. Rejected Unit because behavior spans retry counter/config + terminal error mapping: "After the configured maximum number of retries" then "returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError`". That is a handoff across retry loop and error-construction logic. Rejected System (higher) since no quoted requirement for real network/OS behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries (e.g., 3), stub transport to always return rate-limited failure, run `call()`, and assert rejection is `instanceof QuotaExhaustedError` with expected metadata, not a generic transport error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Top candidate: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejected System because quoted behavior is internal control flow ("retry chain ... terminates correctly" and "does not loop indefinitely") and can be reproduced with in-process stubs, fake timers, and bounded assertions on attempt count/timer queue. No behavior text requires real HTTP/network/OS primitives.
behaviors.test_description: Run orchestrator with always-rate-limited stub and fake clock; advance time through all retries; assert finite completion, exact max-attempt count, and no remaining scheduled retries after terminal error.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Top candidate: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejected System using explicit behavior text: "in the same process" indicates local concurrent module interaction, not external infra dependence. Unit is too low because failure appears at shared-state handoff between multiple callers and quota manager.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/quota bucket, fire concurrent `call()` invocations, and assert aggregate token consumption/enforcement is global (not per-caller), including correct throttling/rejections once shared quota is exhausted.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Top candidate: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejected System because FIFO dispatch ordering is an internal queueing invariant; quoted behavior mentions only "waiting" and "order ... submitted," which is testable with in-process async control and stubs. Unit runner-up is lower but insufficient when queue + scheduler + caller concurrency interact.
behaviors.test_description: Queue several calls while quota is unavailable, then release capacity stepwise; assert transport invocation order matches submission order exactly (FIFO) under concurrent producers.
